## (a) Implementation

```dafny
function Canonicalize(x: Id): Id
{
  if x.width >= PAD then x
  else Id(x.kind, x.value, PAD)
}
```

## (b) Lemma specification

```dafny
lemma Spec(x: Id)
  requires Wellformed(x)
  ensures
    // kind never changes
    Canonicalize(x).kind == x.kind &&
    // numeric value never changes (same entity)
    Canonicalize(x).value == x.value &&
    // result width is at least PAD (the minimum)
    Canonicalize(x).width >= PAD &&
    // result is canonical: width is exactly PAD, unless the value
    // genuinely needs more digits (already >= PAD wide)
    Canonicalize(x).width == (if x.width >= PAD then x.width else PAD) &&
    // result is wellformed
    Wellformed(Canonicalize(x)) &&
    // already-canonical ids are returned unchanged (no-op)
    (x.width >= PAD ==> Canonicalize(x) == x) &&
    // canonicalization is idempotent
    Canonicalize(Canonicalize(x)) == Canonicalize(x)
{ }
```

### Notes on completeness

- **`kind`/`value` preserved**: the two equalities pin down that canonicalization never changes which entity the id refers to.
- **Minimum, not maximum**: `width >= PAD` plus the exact-value clause `width == (if x.width >= PAD then x.width else PAD)` together force padding *up* to 4 only when narrower, and leave already-wide ids untouched (4 is a minimum).
- **No-op on canonical input**: `x.width >= PAD ==> Canonicalize(x) == x` captures that an id already at width ≥ 4 is returned unchanged.
- **Idempotence**: canonicalizing an already-canonical id is a no-op, made explicit via the idempotence clause.
- **Wellformedness preserved**: the result can still hold its value.

The exact-width clause alone determines the full output `Id` (combined with the kind/value preservation), so the spec is both precise and complete; the remaining clauses are entailed consequences stated explicitly for auditability.
